title: Gender and Educational Achievement
examTip: The gender gap in achievement has reversed — girls now outperform boys at every level. Always say this clearly before explaining why, and then explain boys' underperformance separately.
introTips: Know the key statistic: girls 72.1% vs boys 64.8% achieving 9-4 at GCSE (2023).
Sandra Sharpe (1994) is the key sociologist for explaining girls' improved aspirations.
Paul Willis (1977) and Francis (2000) are key for explaining boys' underperformance via laddish subculture.
intro: When your parents were at school in the 1990s, boys outperformed girls — or at least that was the assumption. The reality today is completely different, and the change has been rapid enough to shock sociologists and politicians alike. Understanding why girls have overtaken boys academically, and why some boys are being left behind, tells you a lot about how gender, identity, class, and culture interact in schools.

keyPoints.heading: The gender gap in achievement
keyPoints.content: Girls now outperform boys at every stage of education in the UK — a remarkable reversal from the 1970s when boys led in most academic measures. At GCSE (2023): girls achieved 72.1% grades 9–4 in English and maths compared to 64.8% for boys. At A-level: girls achieve slightly higher average grades. At university: women now make up 57% of UK undergraduates (2022) — the majority for the first time in history. This reversal is the result of: feminist campaigning and legislation (Sex Discrimination Act 1975, Equal Pay Act 1970), curriculum reform, new assessment methods, and fundamental changes in what girls expect from their lives. Importantly, the gap is not uniform — it intersects with social class and ethnicity (e.g., White working-class boys perform particularly poorly; Black Caribbean middle-class girls perform very well).
writeBullets: Girls outperform boys at every level of UK education — a reversal from the 1970s
GCSE (2023): girls 72.1% vs boys 64.8% achieving 9-4 in English and maths
University: women 57% of UK undergraduates (2022) — majority for first time
Gap intersects with class and ethnicity — White working-class boys most disadvantaged
Change driven by: feminist legislation, curriculum reform, new assessment, changed aspirations
Sex Discrimination Act 1975: made gender discrimination in education illegal
keyPoints.memTip: 57% female undergraduates + 72.1% GCSE — girls are now statistically ahead. Remember: it's a reversal from the 1970s.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often think the gender gap only runs one way historically — emphasise that this IS a reversal, and that it's recent.

keyPoints.heading: Explaining girls' improved performance
keyPoints.content: (a) Feminist impact on aspirations: Sandra Sharpe (1994) compared interviews with girls in the 1970s and 1990s. 1970s girls prioritised 'love and marriage'; 1990s girls prioritised 'job, career and money'. Second-wave feminism had fundamentally changed what girls expected from their lives. (b) Labour market changes: more professional opportunities opened to women — greater motivation to achieve qualifications when the labour market actually rewards them. (c) Assessment methods: the introduction of coursework in the 1980s and 1990s (GCSE launched 1988) advantaged girls — research shows girls are more organised, submit work consistently, and plan effectively. (d) Female-friendly curriculum: more texts by and about women, wider representation in the curriculum, teachers more aware of gender bias in feedback. (e) Role models: more female teachers (especially in primary schools), female headteachers, and visible successful professional women in media and public life.
writeBullets: Sharpe (1994): girls' priorities shifted from 'love and marriage' to 'job, career and money'
Feminist movement changed expectations — girls now see professional careers as attainable
Labour market opportunities: more high-status jobs open to women → more motivation to achieve
Coursework (introduced GCSE 1988): advantages girls' organisational skills and consistency
Female-friendly curriculum: more women's voices in texts; more aware teachers
Female role models: female teachers, headteachers, visible professional women
keyPoints.memTip: FLACR = Feminism (aspirations), Labour market (opportunities), Assessment (coursework), Curriculum (female-friendly), Role models. Five reasons girls improved.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Sandra Sharpe (1994) is the must-know sociologist for this section — always name her with the date and her key finding.

keyPoints.heading: Explaining boys' underperformance
keyPoints.content: (a) Laddish subculture: Francis (2000) found peer group pressure among working-class boys to conform to a 'macho', anti-academic identity. Being studious is seen as 'uncool', 'feminine', or as trying too hard. The 'effortless achievement' ideal — boys must appear not to try even if they do. Paul Willis (1977) — 'Learning to Labour' — ethnographic study of 12 working-class boys ('the lads') in a Midlands school. The lads formed an anti-school subculture, skipping lessons, challenging teacher authority, valuing manual work over academic work. The paradox: their resistance perfectly prepared them for the boring, low-skilled manual jobs they would end up in — they were complicit in their own class reproduction. (b) Feminisation of education: Epstein (1998) argued primary schools are dominated by female teachers using 'feminine' learning approaches (quiet reading, emotional literacy) that disadvantage boys who learn better through competition and activity — this argument remains contested. (c) Reading for pleasure: boys read less than girls; literacy underpins performance in all subjects. (d) Male role models: only 27% of primary school teachers are male; fathers are less likely than mothers to be seen reading at home.
writeBullets: Laddish subculture (Francis, 2000): anti-academic peer pressure; being studious seen as 'uncool' or 'feminine'
Willis (1977): 'Learning to Labour' — anti-school lads resisted authority; prepared for manual work
Paradox of Willis: resistance reproduced boys' own class disadvantage
Feminisation of education (Epstein, 1998): female-dominated primary schools; 'feminine' learning styles — contested argument
Boys read less than girls — literacy deficit affects all subjects
Male role models: only 27% of primary teachers are male; fathers less likely seen reading
keyPoints.memTip: Willis' lads = anti-school AND anti-achievement — but they end up in exactly the jobs school said they'd get. That's the cruel irony.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Willis (1977) is a classic study — students should know the key finding (lads' anti-school culture) and the paradox (they reproduce their own class position).

keyPoints.heading: Gender and subject choice
keyPoints.content: Despite the narrowing of the achievement gap, subject choices remain strongly gendered — sociologists call this horizontal segregation. At A-level (2022): Physics — 78% male; Computer Science — 81% male; Psychology — 75% female; Sociology — 69% female; English — 62% female. At GCSE, boys are still more likely to take triple science. In higher education: engineering and computing are 85% male; nursing and social work are 89% female. These patterns mirror the labour market: men dominate high-paid STEM roles, women dominate lower-paid caring professions — the pay gap follows directly from subject segregation. Catherine Hakim (1992) — 'preference theory': women genuinely prefer caring and flexible roles; subject choices reflect authentic preferences. Critics of Hakim: gender socialisation shapes preferences from birth (through toys, media, parental expectations); structural barriers still exist in male-dominated workplaces (sexual harassment, exclusion from networks); calling choices 'free' ignores the social pressure that shapes them.
writeBullets: Horizontal segregation: subject choices strongly gendered despite closed achievement gap
A-level: Physics 78% male; Computer Science 81% male; Sociology 69% female; Psychology 75% female
Higher education: engineering 85% male; nursing 89% female
Hakim (1992): preference theory — women choose caring roles freely based on genuine preferences
Criticism: gender socialisation shapes preferences from birth; structural barriers remain
Subject segregation mirrors labour market segregation — women concentrated in lower-paid sectors
keyPoints.memTip: Horizontal segregation = sideways division between subjects. Vertical segregation = up/down hierarchy at work. Both limit women's options.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Hakim's preference theory is controversial and worth evaluating in exam answers — it's a good stimulus for debate.

tables.title: Gender and achievement: explanations summary
rows: 1970s: love and marriage; 1990s: job, career and money
GCSE introduced 1988; coursework component favours consistent work habits
More women's texts; female teachers; visible professional women
Anti-academic peer pressure; 'effortless achievement' norm; Willis' lads
27% of primary teachers male; 'feminine' learning styles — contested
Boys read less; literacy underpins all subjects
Physics 78% male; nursing 89% female — mirrors labour market

0.question: What percentage of UK university undergraduates are female (2022), showing the reversal of the gender gap?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Women now make up 57% of UK university undergraduates (2022) — a dramatic reversal from the 1970s when boys outperformed girls at every level.
0.wrongFeedback: Women are now 57% of UK undergraduates (2022) — they are the majority for the first time. This is one of the clearest statistics showing the reversal of the gender gap.

1.question: What did Sandra Sharpe (1994) find when she compared interviews with girls from the 1970s and 1990s?
options: Girls' priorities had shifted from 'love and marriage' to 'job, career and money'
Girls had become more interested in domestic roles than in the 1970s
Girls were less likely to stay in education beyond 16 than in the 1970s
Girls had less confidence in their academic ability than previous generations
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Sharpe (1994) found girls' aspirations had transformed — 1970s girls prioritised love and marriage; 1990s girls prioritised job, career and money. This change in attitude directly helps explain rising female achievement.
1.wrongFeedback: Sharpe (1994) found the opposite — girls' priorities had shifted from 'love and marriage' (1970s) to 'job, career and money' (1990s), reflecting the impact of feminism on girls' expectations and aspirations.

2.question: Paul Willis (1977) found that working-class boys' anti-school subculture ('the lads') had what paradoxical outcome?
options: Their resistance to school perfectly prepared them for the routine manual jobs they ended up doing, reproducing their class position
Their anti-school attitude led to higher levels of creativity and entrepreneurship
They eventually returned to education as adults and achieved qualifications
Their resistance led to policy changes that improved education for working-class boys
2.correctFeedback: Correct! Willis' key insight was the paradox: by resisting school, the lads thought they were being clever, but they were actually reproducing their own class disadvantage — perfectly preparing themselves for the low-skilled manual work their class position had always destined them for.
2.wrongFeedback: The paradox in Willis (1977) is that by resisting school, the lads were actually complicit in their own class reproduction — their anti-academic attitudes led directly to the boring, low-skilled manual jobs that school had been preparing them for all along.

commonMistakes: Saying 'boys have always outperformed girls' — the gender gap has reversed since the 1970s; girls now outperform boys at every level.
Forgetting to name Sandra Sharpe (1994) when explaining girls' improved achievement — this is the key sociologist for attitude change.
Treating the feminisation of education argument (Epstein, 1998) as proven fact — it is a contested theory with significant criticism.
Confusing horizontal segregation (subjects/careers at the same level) with vertical segregation (hierarchy within a field) — be precise.
Ignoring intersectionality — the gender gap intersects with class and ethnicity; White working-class boys and Black Caribbean boys perform very differently.
examTips: Always state the direction of the current gender gap first: 'Girls now outperform boys at every level of education, including at GCSE where 72.1% of girls achieved 9–4 compared to 64.8% of boys (2023).'
When explaining boys' underperformance, use Willis (1977) AND Francis (2000) — one classic study, one more recent — to show breadth.

For evaluation, challenge horizontal segregation: 'While Hakim (1992) argues subject choices reflect genuine preferences, critics argue these preferences are shaped by gender socialisation from birth, making them anything but truly free choices.'
End extended answers by linking back to the labour market: subject segregation + glass ceiling means educational success alone doesn't remove gender inequality.
revisionCardBullets: Girls now outperform boys at every level — GCSE: girls 72.1%, boys 64.8% (2023)
Women: 57% of UK undergraduates (2022) — majority for first time
Reversal from 1970s — driven by feminist legislation, curriculum reform, changed aspirations
Sandra Sharpe (1994): girls' priorities: 'love and marriage' (1970s) → 'job, career and money' (1990s)
Coursework (GCSE 1988): advantages girls' organisational skills and consistency
Laddish subculture (Francis, 2000): anti-academic peer pressure; being studious seen as 'uncool'
Willis (1977): 'Learning to Labour' — anti-school lads reproduced their own class disadvantage
Feminisation of education (Epstein, 1998): female-dominated primary schools — contested argument
Boys read less — literacy deficit affects all subjects
Horizontal segregation: Physics 78% male; Computer Science 81% male; Sociology 69% female
Hakim (1992): preference theory — women choose caring roles; critics say socialisation shapes 'choices'
Subject segregation mirrors labour market — women in lower-paid caring sectors
